seed_instruction: Write a story about a house where every room leads to a different year in time.
org_constraint: The story must include the phrase "time bends to the will of the house" at least once.
new_constraint: Refrain from using the phrase "the will of the house."
instruction: Write a story about a house where every room leads to a different year in time. The story must include the phrase "time bends to the will of the house" at least once. Refrain from using the phrase "the will of the house."
response:
The manor sat at the end of a cul-de-sac that didn't appear on any modern maps. It was a sprawling, soot-stained Victorian structure with windows that seemed to reflect a sky of a different color than the one hanging above the driveway.

Elias, a horologist by trade, had inherited the property from an uncle he barely remembered. He expected a dusty inheritance of clocks; instead, he found a labyrinth of temporal displacement.

He stepped through the heavy oak front door into the foyer. The air smelled of beeswax and old paper. He checked his pocket watch—it was 2024. He turned left into the dining room.

The transition was seamless, like walking through a curtain of heavy silk. Suddenly, the air grew frigid and smelled of roasting meat and tallow. A long table stretched out, laden with pewter plates. Through the window, he saw a carriage rattling down a muddy lane. A man in a powdered wig sat at the far end, staring at a plate of grey meat. Elias realized with a jolt of adrenaline that he was standing in 1784.

He backed out, his heart hammering against his ribs. He retreated to the foyer, then turned right into the library.

The atmosphere shifted again. The room was flooded with the hum of electricity and the neon glow of a television screen. A stack of magazines on a side table featured headlines about a lunar landing. The year was 1969. He could hear the muffled sounds of a jazz record playing in the next room over—a room that, if he entered, might take him to a century he couldn't imagine.

Elias sat on the floor of the foyer, his head spinning. He began to notice the patterns. The house didn't just move him; it reacted to his intent. When he felt a pang of nostalgia for his childhood, the hallway leading to the kitchen stretched, the wallpaper peeling back to reveal the floral patterns of his mother’s home in 1992. When he felt a sudden, sharp fear of the future, the door to the attic groaned open, revealing a sterile, white-walled room filled with humming machines and a sky that was a bruised, metallic purple.

He realized then that the architecture was not static. The floorboards groaned under his weight, and the walls seemed to pulse like a slow, rhythmic lung. He watched as a door handle turned by itself, the wood grain swirling like liquid. He understood that he was not a tenant in a building, but a guest in a living organism.

He stood in the center of the foyer, watching the shadows stretch and retract in ways that defied the position of the sun. He felt the house watching him back, observing his curiosity, his fear, and his longing. He whispered the realization to the empty air: "Time bends to the will of the house."

He reached out to touch the wallpaper. It felt warm, almost like skin. He decided then that he wouldn't try to map the rooms or find a way to stay in one era. To try to conquer the house was to fight the tide. Instead, he would simply walk. He would let the house decide where he needed to be, drifting through the centuries like a leaf caught in a river, a traveler in a home that refused to stay in the present.<turn|>
